original_reasoning: Arabic names a tree, and Greek βόλβιτον refers to a bulb or plant growth; the botanical core is plausibly preserved.
Arabic ṣarīḥ can mean pure, unmixed, explicit; Greek means wholly silken, meeting in the core of pure refined material/state.
Arabic gives bending or sinking onto a cushion, and Greek denotes becoming slack or languid; both preserve a clear chain of relaxation and drooping.
Strong harshness can plausibly specialize into boldness and daring.
خزي and ἀκαχίζω meet in the domain of inner distress: shame on the Arabic side and grief or pain on the Greek side.
Greek is perfect passive 'has been left'; Arabic centers on exposure and being left uncovered, which aligns moderately well.
Arabic رزأناه suggests being struck, diminished, or afflicted; Greek passive of ἀάω also carries harm/ruin, giving a plausible suffering chain.
Arabic includes preparing proper speech and invoking God; Greek is propitiation, so both meet in ritual right-ordering and appeasement.
